summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Absolute Dating & Radiometric Methods
summary_sections.content_html:
Absolute (numerical) dating assigns specific ages in years using radioactive decay.

  • Radioactive decay: unstable parent isotopes decay into stable daughter isotopes at a constant rate.
  • Half-life: the time for half of a radioactive sample to decay. Each isotope has a unique half-life.
    • Carbon-14: ~5,730 years (organic material up to ~50,000 years)
    • Potassium-40 → Argon-40: 1.25 billion years (igneous rocks)
    • Uranium-238 → Lead-206: 4.5 billion years (oldest rocks)
  • Scientists measure the parent-to-daughter ratio to calculate age.
  • After 1 half-life: 50% parent remains; after 2: 25%; after 3: 12.5%, etc.
